serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Pork Tripe Stuffed With Meat is approximately 100 g, equivalent to two to three slices of the pressed, braised roll as served in a Chinese deli or restaurant setting.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg per 100 g — far exceeding the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Pork Tripe Stuffed With Meat due to its very high sodium content of 1050 mg/100 g, derived from soy sauce and dark soy sauce in the braising liquid; consuming even a 100 g portion delivers 1050 mg sodium, representing approximately 47% of the 2300 mg daily limit, and the potassium content of 47 mg/100 g provides no meaningful counter-regulatory offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar with high protein; fat content and sodium require monitoring
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pork Tripe Stuffed With Meat contains 0.0 g carbohydrate and 0.0 g sugar per 100 g, resulting in negligible glycaemic impact and making it broadly compatible with blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; however, the fat content of 10.5 g/100 g and very high sodium of 1050 mg/100 g warrant portion control and caution, particularly for individuals with concurrent hypertension or diabetic nephropathy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100 g), cholesterol (89 mg/100 g), and saturated fat profile typical of pork offal
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Pork Tripe Stuffed With Meat only rarely and in small portions, as the combination of high sodium (1050 mg/100 g), cholesterol (89 mg/100 g), and the saturated fat load inherent in pork tripe and pork skin elevates cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation, LDL cholesterol contribution, and endothelial stress; the absence of dietary fibre and antioxidant nutrients further limits any cardioprotective offset.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Double pork product with high arachidonic acid content and very high sodium
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Pork Tripe Stuffed With Meat, as the combination of pork tripe and minced pork provides a high arachidonic acid (omega-6) load that amplifies inflammatory cytokine pathways, while the sodium content of 1050 mg/100 g may exacerbate joint swelling and tissue inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Tripe Stuffed With Meat (罗汉肚, Luóhàn Dù) is a traditional Beijing Chinese delicacy in which a whole pork tripe is meticulously cleaned, then stuffed with a seasoned mixture of minced pork, pork skin, salted egg yolks, and soybeans before being tied, braised in a soy-based liquid, and pressed into a firm, sliceable roll. The dish is served cold or at room temperature in thin slices, revealing a mosaic cross-section of filling ingredients bound within the tripe casing. The texture is dense and chewy from the tripe exterior, with a rich, savoury, and slightly gelatinous interior from the pork skin collagen. The flavour profile is deeply umami, salty, and aromatic from the braising spices. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (19.1 g/100 g) and moderate in fat (10.5 g/100 g), with zero carbohydrate and zero sugar, making it glycaemically neutral. However, the sodium content is very high at 1050 mg/100 g due to the soy sauce and dark soy sauce braising liquid, making it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without strict portion control. Cholesterol is 89 mg/100 g, consistent with pork offal products. The dish is commonly served as a cold appetiser or side dish at lunch and dinner in Chinese restaurants and delis in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 罗汉肚 Pork Tripe Stuffed With Meat (cr_id G00010144) at confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from a primary approved regional source. All core macros are present and internally consistent. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown are not reported in the HPB entry.
notes: carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo (G00010144); this dish contains no starch-based ingredients in the canonical preparation — confirmed zero. sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo — confirmed zero. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo; soybeans contribute negligible fibre in the quantities used — confirmed zero. saturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown (saturated, monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat) not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this dish and not available in USDA FoodData Central for an exact equivalent as of 2026. monounsaturated_fat_g set to -1: same reason as saturated_fat_g. polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: same reason as saturated_fat_g. trans_fat_g set to -1: same reason as saturated_fat_g. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this dish. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100 g); gi_category set to not_applicable per Veritas protocol; glycemic_index field set to numeric 0 per template numeric requirement with gi_category not_applicable signalling inapplicability. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03315595 and FoodEx2 A0CKP are closest_parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for stuffed pork tripe as a prepared dish. Halal status: dish contains pork and is not halal. Gluten status: soy sauce typically contains wheat; dish is not confirmed gluten-free.
